title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at an animal shelter. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Ellen, The Morning Dog Walker
Ellen started walking dogs at the animal shelter after her own terrier died and the house felt too quiet. She expected the routine to be simple, but the mornings were crowded with deliveries, feeding and cleaning. [47]Before any walk could happen, she had to sort out a confusing system of door codes because the kennel gate locked automatically after ten minutes.[/47] More than once, a volunteer was trapped outside in the rain with an impatient dog. [48]Her nursing background helped her record small changes in the animals’ behaviour, such as limping, coughing or refusing food, in a way the staff could quickly understand.[/48] Ellen says the walks taught her to notice happiness in very small forms: a loose lead, a relaxed tail, a dog choosing shade. She now trains new walkers to stay calm, because the animals copy whatever mood the person at the other end of the lead brings.
texts.B:
### Ravi, The Adoption Photographer
[49]Ravi was invited to photograph the shelter animals after the manager saw his portraits at a charity exhibition and asked whether he could make nervous dogs look friendly online.[/49] He said yes before realising how unpredictable the work would be. [50]He was taken aback by the number of consent forms, vaccination records and adoption notes that had to be checked before a single photograph could be published.[/50] At first, he missed the control of a studio, where lights, poses and backgrounds stayed exactly where he put them. In the shelter, cats hid inside blankets and dogs looked away at the wrong moment. Ravi discovered that patience mattered more than technical perfection. His best picture was of an elderly spaniel resting its head on a volunteer’s shoe. The dog was adopted two days later, and Ravi finally understood that a photograph could change an animal’s future. That surprised him quite deeply.
texts.C:
### Grace, The Cat Room Painter
Grace offered to repaint the cat room after spending several Saturdays waiting outside the shelter while her daughter attended training sessions. [51]During those waits, she kept noticing how the dark corridor made the entrance feel gloomy before visitors had even met an animal.[/51] She chose washable colours and painted low stripes that kittens could paw at without leaving obvious marks. [55]She also made large, friendly name cards with a few key facts so visitors could learn each cat’s age and temperament quickly.[/55] [52]The most disappointing moment came when someone scratched a rude message into the fresh paint the very first weekend.[/52] Grace felt foolish for caring so much about a wall, but staff immediately covered the damage and thanked her. She says the project changed how she thinks about welcome: people make decisions with their eyes before anyone speaks, and frightened animals need a room that feels gentle. at once.
texts.D:
### Ben, The Recovery Pen Builder
[53]Ben became a shelter volunteer because his brother had adopted a badly injured stray there, and he wanted to give something back.[/53] As a carpenter, he was asked to improve the recovery pens used after operations. He soon learned that building for animals was not like building furniture. The surfaces had to be easy to disinfect, corners could not trap claws, and every latch had to be opened by humans but not by clever paws. [54]His hardest task was scheduling noisy drilling for the short periods when the veterinary room was empty and recovering animals would not be stressed.[/54] Ben was moved when a cat that had hidden for days finally slept in one of the raised boxes he had made. [56]He is now producing clear instructions and measurements so future volunteers can repair the pens without waiting for him.[/56] He says the best design is the kind nobody notices.

questions.question: mentions a problem with access codes that affected the start of routine work?
questions.explanation: Ellen had to sort out confusing door codes because the kennel gate locked automatically.

questions.question: used a healthcare-related background to record useful observations?
questions.explanation: Ellen’s nursing background helped her record changes in the animals’ behaviour.

questions.question: was invited after someone connected with the organisation saw examples of their work?
questions.explanation: Ravi was invited after the shelter manager saw his portraits at a charity exhibition.

questions.question: was surprised by checks needed before material could be published?
questions.explanation: Ravi was taken aback by the consent forms, records and adoption notes required before publishing photos.

questions.question: noticed a gloomy entrance repeatedly while waiting for someone else?
questions.explanation: Grace noticed the dark corridor while waiting for her daughter at the shelter.

questions.question: felt disappointed after newly completed work was deliberately damaged?
questions.explanation: Someone scratched a rude message into Grace’s fresh paint.

questions.question: joined because of a family member’s connection with an animal helped by the organisation?
questions.explanation: Ben volunteered because his brother had adopted an injured stray there.

questions.question: had to organise noisy work around the needs of vulnerable animals?
questions.explanation: Ben scheduled drilling when the veterinary room was empty and recovering animals would not be stressed.

questions.question: created written information to help visitors understand animals quickly?
questions.explanation: Grace made large name cards with key facts about each cat.

questions.question: is producing instructions so future repairs do not rely on them?
questions.explanation: Ben is producing clear instructions and measurements for future volunteers.
